critique.summary: The article discusses the author's exploration of calendrical calculations related to the coinciding dates of Ramadan, Lent (Ash Wednesday), and Chinese New Year using Emacs Lisp and Common Lisp. The author encounters technical challenges with existing Common Lisp calendar code by Reingold and Dershowitz, modifies it to work with CLISP, and writes scripts to identify rare overlaps of these dates over a 500-year period.
mainPoints: The author initially omitted Ash Wednesday from a previous post about Ramadan and Chinese New Year due to astronomical differences but later recognized cultural connections between Lent and Ramadan.

The author attempted to use Common Lisp code from the book Calendrical Calculations by Reingold and Dershowitz but encountered errors with the original code, which were resolved by removing package-related lines.
Using the fixed calendar code, the author wrote Lisp scripts to find dates where Ramadan 1 and Ash Wednesday coincide over 500 years, identifying several such occurrences.
The author extended the script to also check for Chinese New Year coinciding with Ramadan 1 and Ash Wednesday, finding very rare triple correspondences.
The author notes differences in Lisp implementations, with CLISP being more permissive than SBCL for loading the calendar code.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the author's personal experience with programming and calendrical calculations using publicly available code from the well-known book Calendrical Calculations by Reingold and Dershowitz. The technical challenges and solutions presented align with known issues in Lisp package handling and the use of calendrical algorithms. The dates and coincidences found by the scripts are plausible given the complex nature of lunar and solar calendar interactions. The article does not make unverifiable claims and is consistent with established calendrical computation methods.
considerations: The article assumes familiarity with Lisp programming and calendrical concepts, which may limit accessibility for general audiences.
The cultural significance and variations of Lent, Ramadan, and Chinese New Year are not deeply explored beyond their calendrical coincidence.
The author does not provide external verification or cross-checking of the script outputs with independent calendrical data sources.
The article focuses on technical implementation rather than broader historical or religious context of the holidays.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion focuses on issues with Lisp code related to calendar calculations, specifically around package declarations and code quality. One user provides detailed technical corrections and improvements to the original code, noting non-idiomatic Lisp usage and performance considerations.

comments.author: aidenn0
comments.text:
The cambridge site linked from TFA appears to be hugged-to-death, but I found something that appears to be the same code, under an apache license[1]

I modified that to work under SBCL (but with style-warnings), and fixed up the first example from TFA to actually be free of style-warnings even under sbcl[2]

NB: The code from the book is exceedingly non-idiomatic lisp. In addition, many of the constants violate the (rather strict) ANSI rules around defining constants, so I worked around that by making them special variables (i.e. s/defconstant/defparameter/). A lot could be done to clean things up and allow faster calculations, but finding all ramadan/ash-wednesday correlations in a 5000 (not a typo 10x what TFA does) takes under 1ms on my machine, so shrug

The code from TFA is much, much worse than the code in calendar.l, but there was less of it so I could fix it up a bit more.

1: https://github.com/EdReingold/calendar-code2/blob/main/calen...

2: https://gist.github.com/jasom/1338543679a596449c324eddf716cc...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a knowledgeable critique of the code's style and performance, offering practical fixes and benchmarks, indicating a strong understanding of Lisp and the problem domain.

comments.author: aidenn0
comments.text:
They probably want to replace the header of calendar.l with something roughly like:

  (defpackage "CC4"
    (:use "COMMON-LISP")
    (:export 
     :acre
     :advent
     :akan-day-name
     :akan-day-name-on-or-before

     [and so on for a few hundred lines]
  
     :yom-ha-zikkaron
     :yom-kippur
     :zone
    ))
  
  (in-package "CC4")
Note that :acre could also be 'acre or #:acre or "ACRE"; any string-designator[1] will do. If there are really a few hundred of them then #:acre or "ACRE" has advantages (the other two ways intern symbols).

> believe the problem with the unedited code has something to do with packages and namespaces

Since "package" is what Common Lisp calls namespaces, the "and namespaces' is redundant, but the general thrust is correct.

1: https://www.lispworks.com/documentation/HyperSpec/Body/26_gl...
comments.analysis: This comment correctly explains Lisp package and namespace usage with examples, clarifying terminology and providing a useful link, demonstrating technical accuracy and helpfulness.
